questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: In the given circuit diagram, current in 2 Ω resistor is 2 A , then the current in 6 Ω resistor will be
questions.solution: As in the above branch potential across PQ is 6 V and equivalent resistance is 3 Ω , therefore current in this branch should be 2 A . Therefore voltage across 6 Ω is 4 V and hence current in it is 23 A .
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The total current supplied to the circuit by the battery is
questions.solution:
Let us assume that the battery of  6 V is connected across points A and B. Let the centre point be C.

Using the point-potential method, the points having the same potential should have the same name.

After redrawing the circuit, the figure looks as shown below,

 

The current supplied by the battery is

i=VRAB

V=6 V.

Now, to find the equivalent resistance between A and B,

RAB=3∥RAC+RCB

RAC=6×26+2

RAC=1.5 Ω

RBC=1.5 Ω

RAB=3∥1.5+1.5

RAB=3∥3

RAB=3×33+3

RAB=1.5 Ω.

Thus, the net resistance between A and B is 1.5 Ω.

Now, the current flowing through the battery is

i=61.5 A.

Hence, the current flowing through the battery is 4 A.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: When a resistor of $11 \Omega$ is connected in series with an electric cell, the current flowing in it is 0.5 A. Instead, when a resistor of $5 \Omega$ is connected to the same electric cell in series, the current increases by $0.4 \mathrm{~A}$. The internal resistance of the cell is
questions.solution:
Current taken from the cell, $i=\frac{E}{R+r}$
where $R=$ external resistance and
$r=$ internal resistance
$\begin{aligned} \text { Ist Case } i_1 & =\frac{E}{R_1+r} \\ 0.5 & =\frac{E}{11+r}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \text { IInd Case } 0.5+0.4=\frac{E}{5+r} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 0.9=\frac{E}{5+r}\end{aligned}$
Dividing Eq. (ii) by (i),
$\begin{aligned} \frac{0.5}{0.9} & =\frac{\frac{E}{(11+r)}}{\frac{E}{(5+r)}} \\ \frac{5}{9} & =\frac{5+r}{11+r} \\ 55+5 r & =45+9 r \\ 10 & =4 r \Rightarrow r=2.5 \Omega\end{aligned}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A galvanometer of resistance $50 \Omega$ is connected to a battery of $3 \mathrm{~V}$ along with a resistance of $2950 \Omega$ in series. A full scale deflection of 30 divisions is obtained in the galvanometer. In order to reduce this deflection to 20 divisions, the resistance in series should be
questions.solution:
Current through the galvanometer
$$ \begin{aligned} I=\frac{3}{(50+2950)} &=10^{-3} \mathrm{~A} \\ \text { Current for } 30 \text { divisions } &=10^{-3} \mathrm{~A} \\ \text { Current for } 20 \text { divisions } &=\frac{10^{-3}}{30} \times 20 \\ &=\frac{2}{3} \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~A} \end{aligned} $$ For the same deflection to obtain for 20 divisions, let resistance added be $R$ $$ \begin{array}{lc} \therefore \quad \frac{2}{3} \times 10^{-3}=\frac{3}{(50+1 R)} \\ \text { or } \quad R=4450 \Omega \end{array} $$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A wire of length l has a resistance R. If half of the length is stretched to make the radius half of its original value, then the final resistance of the wire is
questions.solution:
R= ρl A = ρl π r 2

π r 2 l 2 =π ( r 2 ) 2 l ′

l ′ =2l

R'=ρl'A'=ρ2lπr22=8R

R''=ρl2πr2=R2

Req=R'+R''=17R2
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Two resistances of $400 \Omega$ and $800 \Omega$ are connected in series with $6 \mathrm{~V}$ battery of negligible internal resistance. A voltmeter of resistance $10000 \Omega$ is used to measure the potential difference across $400 \Omega$. The error in the measurement of potential difference in volts approximately is :
questions.solution:
$R_1=400 \Omega, R_2=800 \Omega$

$P D$ across $400 \Omega$ resistance (when voltmeter is not connected) $V_1=\frac{6}{(400+800)} \times 400$ $=\frac{6 \times 400}{1200}=2 \mathrm{~V}$ when voltmeter is connected Total resistance of the circuit, $R=\left(\frac{10000 \times 400}{10000+400}\right)+800$ $=\frac{10000 \times 400}{10400}+800$ $=\frac{40000}{104}+800$ $=\frac{40000+83200}{104}$ $=\frac{123200}{104}=\frac{30800}{26}=\frac{15400}{13}$ New $P D$ across $400 \Omega$ resistance, $V_2=6-800\left(\frac{6}{15400 / 13}\right)$ $=6-\frac{800 \times 13 \times 6}{15400}$ $=6-4.052=1.95 \mathrm{~V}$ $\therefore \quad$ Error $=V_1-V_2$ $=2-1.95$ $=0.05$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A conductor of resistance $3 \Omega$ is stretched uniformly till its length is doubled. The wire is now bent in the form of an equilateral triangle. The effective resistance between the ends of any side of the triangle in ohms is
questions.solution:
Resistance $R \propto l^2$
$$ \begin{aligned} \frac{R_1}{R_2} & =\left(\frac{l_1}{l_2}\right)^2 \\ \frac{3}{R_2} & =\left(\frac{l}{2 l}\right)^2 \\ \Rightarrow \quad R_2 & =12 \Omega \end{aligned} $$ Stretched wire is bent in the form of an equilateral triangle. $\therefore$ Resistance of each side $=\frac{12}{3}=4 \Omega$ $$ R_1=R_2=R_3=4 \Omega $$ Equivalent resistance of $R_1$ and $R_2$ (in series) $$ \begin{aligned} R^{\prime} & =R_1+R_2 \\ & =4+4=8 \Omega \end{aligned} $$ Now, $R^{\prime}$ and $R_3$ are in parallel so total resistance $$ \begin{aligned} R & =\frac{R^{\prime} R_3}{R^{\prime}+R_3}=\frac{8 \times 4}{8+4} \\ & =\frac{32}{12}=\frac{8}{3} \Omega \end{aligned} $$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Two batteries of emf E1 and E2 E2>E1 and internal resistances r1 and r2 respectively are connected in parallel as shown in the figure. Then, which of the following statements is correct?
options: The equivalent emf <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> is smaller than <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>.
The equivalent emf <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> is greater than <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>.

The equivalent emf <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> of two cells is between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> always.
questions.solution:
The equivalent internal resistance of the two cells between A and B is

R=r1r2r1+r2

If E is the equivalent emf of the two cells in parallel, then

ER=E1r1+E2r2=E1r2+E2r1r1r2
∴E=(E1r2+E2r1r1r2)R=E1r2+E2r1r1+r2
As, E2>E1⇒E1<E<E2  [For all values of  r1 and r2
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> bulb and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> bulb are joined in series and connected to the mains. Which bulb will glow brighter -
options: First <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math> bulb and then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math> bulb
Both will glow with same brightness
questions.solution:
(Brightness) ∝ (Consumed power) .....(i)

For series combination

Pconsumed = i2R

∴Pconsumed ∝R ......(ii)

Also, Rated Power

PRated = V2R

PRated ∝ 1R .....(iii

From, (i), (ii) and (iii)

Brightness∝ 1Prated

∴ 25 W bulb will glow brighter.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Three unequal resistors in parallel are equivalent to a resistance $1 \mathrm{ohm}$. If two of them are in the ratio $1: 2$ and if no resistance value is fractional, then the largest of the three resistances in ohms is
questions.solution:
Let the three resistances are $R_1, R_2$ and $R_3$.
$\therefore \quad \frac{R_1}{R_2}=\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow R_1=k, R_2=2 k$
In parallel, $\quad \frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{R_1}+\frac{1}{R_2}+\frac{1}{R_3}$
 $\frac{1}{1}=\frac{1}{k}+\frac{1}{2 k}+\frac{1}{R_3}$
$\frac{1}{R_3}=1-\frac{1}{k}-\frac{1}{2 k}$
$=\frac{2 k-2-1}{2 k}=\frac{2 k-3}{2 k}$
$R_3=\frac{2 k}{2 k-3}$
If $k=1$, then $R_3$ is found to be negative, which is impossible.
If $k=2$, then $R_1=2, R_2=4, R_3=4$
$R_2=R_3$, not satisfying the condition of the question that all resistance are unequal.
If $k=3$, then $R_1=3, R_2=6$ 
$R_3=2 \Omega$
$\therefore \quad$ Largest resistance $=6 \Omega$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Every atom makes one free electron in copper. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> Current is flowing in the wire of copper having <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> diameter, then the drift velocity (approx.) will be (density of copper <math><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3 </mn></mrow></msup></math>and atomic weight of copper  = <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>63</mn></math>)
questions.solution:
v⁡d⁡=i⁡n⁡Ae;  
where n⁡=NAρM⁡

=6.023×1026×9×10363=0.860×1029

=8.6×1028

and A=πD24=227×10-324 m2;

=1114×10-6 m2

vd=1.18.6×1028×1114×10-6×1.6 ×10-19

=19.6×10+3=100×10-496=1.0×10-4 m s-1

=0.1 mm s-1
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
A galvanometer, having a resistance of $50 \Omega$, gives a full scale deflection for a current of 0.05 A. The length in metre of a resistance wire of area of cross-section $2.97 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~cm}^2$ that can be used to convert the galvanometer into an ammeter which can read a maximum of 5 A current is :
(Specific resistance of the wire$\left.=5 \times 10^{-7} \Omega-\mathrm{m}\right)$
questions.solution:
Resistance of galvanometer,
$G=50 \Omega$
Full scale current, $i_g=0.05$
$A=2.97 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~cm}^2$
$=2.97 \times 10^{-2} \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^2$
$=2.97 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}^2$
$i=5 \mathrm{~A}$
$\rho=5 \times 10^{-7} \Omega-\mathrm{m}$
Required resistance to convert the galvanometer into ammeter,
$R=\frac{i_g G}{i-i_g}=\frac{0.05 \times 50}{5-0.05}=\frac{25}{4.95}$
$\rho \frac{l}{A}=\frac{50}{99}$
$l=\frac{50}{99} \times \frac{A}{\rho}$
$=\frac{50}{99} \times \frac{2.97 \times 10^{-6}}{5 \times 10^{-7}}$
$=\frac{50}{99} \times \frac{29.7}{5}$
$=10 \times 0.3$
$=3 \mathrm{~m}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The current enters at A and comes out at D. Some of the resistances are shown. What should be resistance of wire CB so that it draws double of the current that enters the wire BF.
questions.solution:
Let current in AB be I1, in BF be I2, in BC be 2I2, in FC be I3, in FE be I3 and in CD be I3+2I2,

at point B, I1=3I2, at point, F, I2=2I3

First, convert the circuit in simple form and using current law given by Kirchhoff's, 

Along, ABCD, 

VA-VD=V=I12R5+I2R5+I3R5+I3+2I2R5=2I2R

Along, ABCD,

VA-VD=V=2I2R=I12R5+2I2R'+I3+2I2R5

⇒2I2R'=310I2R⇒R'=320×R
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A copper wire of <math><mn>3</mn><mi> </mi><mn>m</mn><msup><mrow><mn>m</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> cross-sectional area carries a current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>. The magnitude of the drift velocity for the electrons in the wire is (Assume each copper atom contributes one free electron and density of copper <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>8920</mn><mi> </mi><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mn>m</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>M</mi><mi>Cu</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>63</mn><mo>.5</mo><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
questions.solution:
Given, A=3×10-6 m2, I=5 A,

MCu=63.5 kg mol-1

ρCu=8920 kg m-3

Electron density =6.023×1023×892063.5

=8.46×1025 m-3

∴ Drift velocity, vd=IenA

=51.6×10-19×8.46×1025×3×10-6

=540.608

=0.12 m s-1
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The figure shows a network in which the cell is ideal and it has an emf E. The potential difference across the resistance 2R is
questions.solution:
In the given circuit, resistors 4R and 2R are connected in parallel while resistance R is connected in series to it.

Hence, equivalent resistance is, of parallel combination 
1R′=14R+12R

⇒ R'=86R =43R 

⟹ R" =R+43R=7R3(series combination)

Given, emf is E volts, thereforeusing ohm's law,

i=ER''=3E7R
    Potential difference across R is

V=ir=3E7R×R=3E7

Potential difference across 2R is

V′=E-3E7=4E7
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A battery of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is connected to a resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> through a variable resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math>. If the variable resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is increased at the rate of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>min</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, then the amount of charge that passes through the battery in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>min</mi></math> is
questions.solution:
Q= ∫i dt = ∫VRdt= ∫02401020+560tdt     t converted in seconds

=10560 ln⁡560t+200240

=6005ln⁡4020=120ln⁡2
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: In the part of the circuit shown in the figure, the potential difference between points VA-VB=16 V. The current passing through the 2 Ω resistance will be
questions.solution:
VA-VB=16 V
∴4i1+2i1+i2-3+4i1=16 V     ......(i)

Using Kirchhoff's second law in the closed loops we have
⇒9-i2-2i1+i2=0                  ......(ii)

Solving equations (i) and (ii), we get
i1=1.5 A and i2=2 A

∴  Current through 2 Ω resistor =2+1.5=3.4 A
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: In a meter bridge a $30 \Omega$ resistance is connected in the left gap and a pair of resistances $P$ and $Q$ in the right gap. Measured from the left, the balance point is $37.5 \mathrm{~cm}$, when $P$ and $Q$ are in series and $71.4 \mathrm{~cm}$ they are in parallel. The values of $P$ and $Q$ (in $\Omega$ ) are
questions.solution:
Ist case $\begin{aligned} & \frac{30}{P+Q}=\frac{l}{(100-l)} \\ & \frac{30}{P+Q}=\frac{37.5}{(100-37.5)} \\ & \frac{30}{P+Q}=\frac{37.5}{62.5} \\ & P+Q=\frac{30 \times 62.5}{37.5} \end{aligned}$
IInd case $\begin{aligned} \frac{30}{\frac{P Q}{P+Q}} & =\frac{l}{(100-l)} \\ \frac{30(P+Q)}{P Q} & =\frac{71.4}{(100-71.4)} \\ \frac{30 \times 50}{P Q} & =\frac{71.4}{28.6} \\ P Q & =\frac{30 \times 50 \times 28.6}{71.4} \end{aligned}$
So, from Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get $P=30 \Omega, Q=20 \Omega$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: In the given electrical circuit, which one of the following equation is a correct equation?
questions.solution:
The given circuit can be drawn as

Applying Kirchhoff’s voltage law (KVL) in loop ABCD, we get
-i1+i2R+E2-i2r2=0 …. (i)
Applying KVL in loop ABFEA, we get
-i1+i2R-i1r1+E1=0 ….. (ii)
Applying KVL in Loop EFCDE, we get
-E1+i1r1+E2-i2r2=0 ….. (iii)
From the given options, only option (c) satisfies Eq. (ii), hence it is the correct equation.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Consider a cylindrical conductor of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> and area of cross-section <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>. The specific conductivity varies as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">σ</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">σ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><msqrt><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></msqrt></mfrac></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is the distance along the axis of the cylinder from one of its ends. The resistance of the system along the cylindrical axis is
questions.solution:
Given, σx=σ01x

∵ Resistance of the system along the cylindrical axis,

R=∫0LρxAdx

= ∫0L1σ0LxAdx ∵ρx=1σx

= ∫0Lxσ0ALdx=1σ0ALx32320L=231σ0ALL32-0

=23⋅1σ0AL×L32, R=23⋅LAσ0
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A uniform metal wire carries a current of $2 \mathrm{~A}$ when an ideal cell of $3.4 \mathrm{~V}$ is connected across it. The wire has mass $8.92 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~kg}$, density $8.92 \times 10^3 \mathrm{kgm}^{-3}$ and resistivity $1.7 \times 10^{-8} \Omega \mathrm{m}$. Then the length of the wire is
questions.solution:
Current, $\mathrm{I}=2 \mathrm{~A}$
Potential, $\Delta V=3.4 \mathrm{v}$
Using Ohm's law
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{R}=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{I}}=\frac{3.4}{2}=1.7 \Omega \\
& \text { Volume }=\frac{\text { Mass }}{\text { Density }} \\
& =\frac{8.92 \times 10^{-3}}{8.92 \times 10^3}=10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}^3 \\
& \mathrm{R}=\frac{\rho \mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{VA}} \times \frac{\mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{L}}=\frac{\rho \mathrm{L}^2}{\mathrm{~V}} \\
& \mathrm{~L}^2=\frac{\mathrm{RV}}{\rho}=\frac{1.7 \times 10^{-6}}{1.7 \times 10^{-8}}
\end{aligned}$
Length of the wire, $\mathrm{L}=10 \mathrm{~m}$.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Two resistances of $400 \Omega$ and $800 \Omega$ are connected in series with $6 \mathrm{~V}$ battery of negligible internal resistance. A voltmeter of resistance $10000 \Omega$ is used to measure the potential difference across $400 \Omega$. The error in the measurement of potential difference in volts approximately is :
questions.solution:
$R_1=400 \Omega, R_2=800 \Omega$

$P D$ across $400 \Omega$ resistance (when voltmeter is not connected) $V_1=\frac{6}{(400+800)} \times 400$ $=\frac{6 \times 400}{1200}=2 \mathrm{~V}$ when voltmeter is connected Total resistance of the circuit, $R=\left(\frac{10000 \times 400}{10000+400}\right)+800$ $=\frac{10000 \times 400}{10400}+800$ $=\frac{40000}{104}+800$ $=\frac{40000+83200}{104}$ $=\frac{123200}{104}=\frac{30800}{26}=\frac{15400}{13}$ New $P D$ across $400 \Omega$ resistance, $V_2=6-800\left(\frac{6}{15400 / 13}\right)$ $=6-\frac{800 \times 13 \times 6}{15400}$ $=6-4.052=1.95 \mathrm{~V}$ $\therefore \quad$ Error $=V_1-V_2$ $=2-1.95$ $=0.05$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
The deflection in moving coil galvanometer of resistance $45 \Omega$ falls from
30 divisions to 3 divisions. The length of the shunt wire required to convert
galvanometer to ammeter is
[specific resistance of material of shunt wire $=5 \times 10^{-7} \Omega \mathrm{m}$ and area of cross-
section of wire $\left.=4 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m}^{2}\right]$
questions.solution:
As per the Question,
$I_{g}=\frac{3}{30} I=\frac{1}{10} I_{,}$
$\Rightarrow\left(I-I_{G}\right) S=I_{G}G$
$\Rightarrow(I-\frac{1}{10} I) S=\frac{1}{10}I G$
$\Rightarrow (\frac{9}{10}) I S$ $=(\frac{1}{10})I G$
$\Rightarrow 9 S=G$
$\therefore G=45 \quad \therefore S=5$
$\therefore S=\frac{S L}{A}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{S A}{S}=L=4 \mathrm{~m}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Twelve cells, each having emf $E$ volts are connected in series and are kept in a closed box. Some of these cells are wrongly connected with positive and negative terminals reversed. This 12 cell battery is connected in series with an ammeter, an external resistance $R$ ohms and a two-cell battery (two cells of the same type used earlier, connected perfectly in series). The current in the circuit when the 12-cell battery and 2-cell battery aid each other is 3A and is $2 \mathrm{~A}$ when they oppose each other. Then, the number of cells in 12-cell battery that are connected wrongly is :
questions.solution:
Let polarity of $m$ cells in a 12 cells battery is reversed, then equivalent emf of the battery $=(12-2 m) E$
Now the circuit can be drawn as : When 12-cell battery and 2-cell battery aid each other, then current through the circuit, $i_1=\frac{(12-2 m) E+2 E}{R}$ or $\quad 3=\frac{(14-2 m) E}{R}$ ...(i) When they oppose each other, the current through the circuit. $i_2=\frac{(12-2 m) E-2 E}{R}$ or $\quad 2=\frac{(10-2 m) E}{R}$ ...(ii) Dividing Eq. (i) by (ii), we have $\frac{3}{2}=\frac{14-2 m}{10-2 m}$ or $\quad 30-6 m=28-4 m$ or $\quad 2 m=2$ $\therefore \quad m=1$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: In the circuit given below, if the bulb is to glow with maximum intensity, the value of ' $R$ ' is (neglect internal resistance of the cell)
questions.solution:
Resistance of bulb, $R=\frac{V^2}{P}$ $=\frac{(1.5)^2}{0.45}=5 \Omega$
$P=i_1^2 R$ $\begin{aligned} & 0.45=\mathrm{i}_1{ }^2 5 \\ & \mathrm{i}_1=0.3 \mathrm{~A}\end{aligned}$ Apply KVL, We have $\begin{aligned} & -6+3 \mathrm{i} 1.5=0 \\ & \mathrm{i}=\frac{6-1.5}{3}=1.5 \mathrm{~A} \\ & 1.5=\left(\mathrm{i}-\mathrm{i}_1\right) \mathrm{R} \\ & 1.5=(1.5-0.3) \mathrm{R} \\ & \mathrm{R}=\frac{1.5}{1.2}=1.25 \Omega\end{aligned}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.q: The charge on the 4μF capacitor in the steady state is
questions.solution:
In steady state, the capacitors are fully charged and act as open circuit, so the equivalent circuit in steady state would be as shown:-


∴ Steady State current is I=122+4=2 A
∴ Potential difference across AB is V=2×4=8 V
∴ Sum of potential difference across 2 μF and 4 μF capacitors is 8 V. As these are in series, so charges on them would be same. Let q be the charge on them, then:-
From KVL, 

q2+q4=8⇒q=323 μC

When the two cells of e.m.f.s $1.3 \mathrm{~V}$ and $1.1 \mathrm{~V}$ are connected so as to assist each other and then oppose each other, the balancing lengths are respectively

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A resistance network is connected to a battery as shown in the figure below. If the internal resistance of the battery is $5 \Omega$, then the value of $R$ (in $\Omega$ ) for maximum power delivered to the network is
questions.solution:
Given is an unbalanced Wheatstones' bridge, in which current distribution will be as shown
Now in loop $A B C A$, using KVL we have, $$ \begin{aligned} & -3 R i_1-4 R\left(i_1-i_2\right)+2 R i_2=0 \\ & -3 R i_1-4 R i_1+4 R i_2+2 R i_2=0 \\ & 7 i_1=6 i_2 \\ & \Rightarrow \\ & i_1=\frac{6}{7} i_2 \\ & \end{aligned} $$ Also, $\quad i_1+i_2=i$ Hence, $\left(\frac{6}{7}+1\right) i_2=i$ or $i_2=\frac{7}{13} i$ $$ \therefore \quad i_1=\frac{6}{7} i_2=\frac{6}{7} \times \frac{7}{13} i \Rightarrow i_1=\frac{6}{13} i $$ Now from loop $A B D A$ (which includes cell), we have, $3 R i_1+2 R i_2 \approx V$ (nearly because internal resistance in not taken in account) $$ 3 R \times \frac{6}{13} i+2 R \times \frac{7}{13} i \approx i R_{\mathrm{eq}} $$ $$ \Rightarrow \quad R_{\mathrm{eq}}=R\left(\frac{18+14}{13}\right)=\frac{32}{13} R $$ For maximum power delivered to circuit, (internal resistance of source $)=($ external resistance) $$ \Rightarrow \quad 5=\frac{32 R}{13} \text { or } R=\frac{13 \times 5}{32}=203 \Omega $$ or $R \approx 2 \Omega$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Figure shows an infinite ladder network of resistances. The equivalent resistance between points X and Y is
questions.solution:
Let Req be the equivalent resistance of this network.
⇒Req=1+Req×11+Req
⇒R+R2=1+R+R
⇒R2-R-1=0
⇒R=1±1+4×12×1
⇒R = 1±52
⇒R =1.62 Ω
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A battery of emf $10 \mathrm{~V}$ is connected to a uniform wire $A B$ of $1 \mathrm{~m}$ length and having a resistance of $10 \Omega$ in series with a $10 \Omega$ resistor as shown in the figure. Two cells of emf $2 \mathrm{~V}$ and $3 \mathrm{~V}$ having internal resistance $2 \Omega$ and $3 \Omega$, respectively are connected as shown in the figure. If the galvanometer shows null deflection at point $J$ on the wire, then the distance of point $J$ from the point $B$ is.
questions.solution:
Given $r=10 \Omega, R=10 \Omega, E=10 \mathrm{~V}$ and $L=100 \mathrm{~cm}$. Now, voltage drop on the wire, $E^{\prime}=\frac{E}{(r+R)} \times R$ $$ \Rightarrow \quad E^{\prime}=\frac{10}{20} \times 10=5 R $$ So, potential gradient on wire,
Effective emf of combination in secondary circuit, $$ \begin{array}{rlrl} & & \frac{V}{r_{\text {eff }}} & =\frac{\varepsilon_1}{r_1}+\frac{\varepsilon_2}{r_2} \\ \Rightarrow \text { Here, } \quad & \frac{1}{r_{\text {eff }}} & =\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{3}=\frac{5}{6} \\ \Rightarrow \quad & V & =\frac{6}{5}(1+1)=\frac{12}{5} \end{array} $$ Now, at balancing point (from point $A$ ) is $$ \begin{aligned} & l=V / x=\frac{12}{5} \times \frac{100}{5} \\ & l=48 \mathrm{~cm} \end{aligned} $$ So, length from point $B$ is $$ 100-48=52 \mathrm{~cm} $$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Find the potential difference between a and b, as shown in the below circuit.
questions.solution:
Equivalent for parallel combination of 5 Ω & 15 Ω is 

Req1=15×515+5=154 Ω

Similarly for three parallel resistor's equivalent 

1Req 2=124+112+18  ⇒  Req 2=4 Ω

Now current through 45 V branch,

I=45Req 1=4515/4=12 A

Voltage across a & b is

V=Req×I=4+10+154×12=213 V
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A cell of emf <math><mi>E</mi></math> and internal resistance <math><mi>r</mi></math> supplies currents for the same time <math><mi>t</mi></math> through external resistance <math><msub><mrow><mi>R</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo></math>100 <math><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi> </mi><msub><mrow><mi>R</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>40</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> separately. If the heat developed in both cases is the same, then the internal resistance of the cell is given by
questions.solution:
Current drawn from the cell in resistance R1 will be I=E/(R1+r)

Therefore, the heat produced in R1 i.e.

H1=E2R1tR1+r2

Heat produced in R2 , H2=E2R2tR2+r2

As per question H1=H2

or E2R1tR1+r2= E2R2tR2+r2

On solving we get;

r=R1R2

=100×40=63.25 Ω
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
Charge passing through a conductor of cross-section $0.3 \mathrm{~m}^2$ is given by $\mathrm{q}=\left(3 \mathrm{t}^2+5 \mathrm{t}+2\right) \mathrm{C}$ where ' $\mathrm{t}$ ' is in seconds. The drift velocity at $t=2 \mathrm{~s}$ is
(Concentration of electrons in the conductor $=2 \times 10^{25} \mathrm{~m}^{-3}$ )
questions.solution:
Given, area of cross-section $\mathrm{A}=0.3 \mathrm{~m}^2$,
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{n}=2 \times 10^{25} \mathrm{~m}^{-3} \text { charge, } \mathrm{q}=\left(3 \mathrm{t}^2+5 \mathrm{t}+2\right) \mathrm{C}, \\
& \mathrm{t}=2 \mathrm{~s}, \mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{d}}=? \\
& \mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{d}}=\frac{\mathrm{i}}{\mathrm{neA}}=\frac{\mathrm{dq} / \mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{neA}}\left[\because \mathrm{i}=\frac{\mathrm{dq}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right] \\
& \mathrm{i}=\frac{\mathrm{dq}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dt}}\left(3 \mathrm{t}^2+5 \mathrm{t}+2\right)=6 \mathrm{t}+5=6 \times 2+5 \\
& =17 \\
& \therefore \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{d}}=\frac{\mathrm{i}}{\mathrm{neA}}=\frac{17}{2 \times 10^{25} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.3} \\
& =\frac{17}{0.96 \times 10^6}=1.77 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}
\end{aligned}
$$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: In the circuit, $E_1=E_2=E_3=2 V$ and $R_1$ and $\mathrm{R}_2=4 \mathrm{~W}$. Then the current flowing through $\mathrm{E}_2$ is
questions.solution: $$ \begin{aligned} & E_{e q}=\frac{\frac{E_1}{R_1}+\frac{E_3}{R_2}}{\frac{1}{R_1}+\frac{1}{R_2}}=\frac{E_1 R_2+E_3 R_1}{R_1+R_2}=\frac{2 \times 4+2 \times 4}{42+4}=2 V \\ & R_{e q}=\frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1+R_2}=\frac{4 \times 4}{4 \times 4}=2 \Omega \\ & -E_2+i R_{e q}-E_{e q}=0 \\ & i=\frac{E_2+E_{e q}}{R_{e q}}=\frac{2+2}{2}=2 A \end{aligned} $$ Hence, the current flowing through $\mathrm{E}_2$ is $2 \mathrm{~A}$ from $\mathrm{A}$ to $\mathrm{B}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Two cells $P$ and $Q$ each of emf $2.16 \mathrm{~V}$ are connected in series with a resistor of $19.6 \Omega$. An ideal voltmeter reads $2 \mathrm{~V}$, when connected across the cell $P$ and $1.92 \mathrm{~V}$ when connected across the cell $Q$. The ratio of the internal resistances of the cell $P$ and $Q$ is
questions.solution:
Emf of each cell $=2.16 \mathrm{~V}$ Emf of circuit $=4.32 \mathrm{~V}$ Current in circuit, $$ I=\frac{4.32}{r_1+r_2+19.6} $$ Now, using $V=E-I \cdot r$ for both cells, we have For $P, 2=2.16-\frac{4.32 r_1}{r_1+r_2+19.6}$ and For $Q, 1.92=216-\frac{4.32 r_2}{r_1+r_2+19.6}$ So, we have,
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get $$ r_1: r_2=2: 3 $$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
Assertion: Resistivity of conductor and semiconductors increases with temperature.

Reason: The electrons move with more kinetic energy and hence relaxation time decreases.
options: If both Assertion and Reason are true and the Reason is correct explanation of the Assertion.
If both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is not explanation of the Assertion.
If Assertion is true but the Reason is false.
If Assertion is false but Reason is true.
questions.solution:
Resistivity of conductor increases and that of semiconductors decreases with temperature.
Higher kinetic energy of electrons refer to higher number collisions per unit time.
So, Assertion is false but Reason is true.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
